name: utils

members.name: clip_grad_norm_
members.signature: clip_grad_norm_(parameters: Iterable[Parameter], max_norm: float, norm_type: float = 2.0, error_if_nonfinite: bool = False)
members.summary: Clip the global gradient norm of `parameters` in place.
members.extended:
Rescales every gradient so that the total $\ell_p$ norm —
computed across all parameters jointly, as if they were one long
concatenated vector — is at most `max_norm`.  A staple of stable
Transformer / RNN training: prevents the occasional huge gradient
from derailing optimisation.

parameters.name: parameters
parameters.description:
Parameters whose `.grad` should be clipped.  Entries with
`grad is None` are silently skipped.

parameters.name: max_norm
parameters.description:
Maximum allowed norm of the combined gradient vector.  The
scaling factor never exceeds `1` — gradients smaller than
`max_norm` are untouched.

parameters.name: norm_type
parameters.description:
Order $p$ of the norm.  Default `2.0` (Euclidean).  Pass
`math.inf` for the max-norm (element-wise absolute maximum
across all gradients).

parameters.name: error_if_nonfinite
parameters.description:
If `True`, raise `RuntimeError` when the computed total
norm is `inf` or `nan` instead of silently scaling by a
non-finite coefficient.

returns.description:
Scalar tensor holding the *pre-clipping* total norm.  Useful for
logging the gradient magnitude during training even when no
actual clipping took place.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import clip_grad_norm_
>>> # after loss.backward() ...
>>> total_norm = clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
notes:
With combined norm $\|g\|_p = \left(\sum_i |g_i|^p\right)^{1/p}$
taken over every element of every gradient, the update is



$$
g \;\mapsto\; g \cdot \min\!\left(1,\,
    \frac{\text{max\_norm}}{\|g\|_p + \epsilon}\right),
$$

where the $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ guards against division by zero
when all gradients vanish.  Because every parameter is scaled by the
*same* coefficient the direction of the global update is preserved —
only its magnitude is bounded.

members.name: clip_grad_norm
members.signature: clip_grad_norm_(parameters: Iterable[Parameter], max_norm: float, norm_type: float = 2.0, error_if_nonfinite: bool = False)
members.summary: Clip the global gradient norm of `parameters` in place.
members.extended:
Rescales every gradient so that the total $\ell_p$ norm —
computed across all parameters jointly, as if they were one long
concatenated vector — is at most `max_norm`.  A staple of stable
Transformer / RNN training: prevents the occasional huge gradient
from derailing optimisation.

parameters.name: parameters
parameters.description:
Parameters whose `.grad` should be clipped.  Entries with
`grad is None` are silently skipped.

parameters.name: max_norm
parameters.description:
Maximum allowed norm of the combined gradient vector.  The
scaling factor never exceeds `1` — gradients smaller than
`max_norm` are untouched.

parameters.name: norm_type
parameters.description:
Order $p$ of the norm.  Default `2.0` (Euclidean).  Pass
`math.inf` for the max-norm (element-wise absolute maximum
across all gradients).

parameters.name: error_if_nonfinite
parameters.description:
If `True`, raise `RuntimeError` when the computed total
norm is `inf` or `nan` instead of silently scaling by a
non-finite coefficient.

returns.description:
Scalar tensor holding the *pre-clipping* total norm.  Useful for
logging the gradient magnitude during training even when no
actual clipping took place.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import clip_grad_norm_
>>> # after loss.backward() ...
>>> total_norm = clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
notes:
With combined norm $\|g\|_p = \left(\sum_i |g_i|^p\right)^{1/p}$
taken over every element of every gradient, the update is



$$
g \;\mapsto\; g \cdot \min\!\left(1,\,
    \frac{\text{max\_norm}}{\|g\|_p + \epsilon}\right),
$$

where the $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ guards against division by zero
when all gradients vanish.  Because every parameter is scaled by the
*same* coefficient the direction of the global update is preserved —
only its magnitude is bounded.

members.name: clip_grad_value_
members.signature: clip_grad_value_(parameters: Iterable[Parameter], clip_value: float)
members.summary: Clamp every gradient element to $[-\text{clip\_value}, \text{clip\_value}]$ in place.
members.extended:
Unlike `clip_grad_norm_`, which preserves the direction of the
full gradient vector, this operates element-wise — each scalar entry
is independently clipped to the symmetric interval.  Cheap, and a
useful safety net when only a handful of weights tend to blow up
(e.g. embedding tables on rare tokens).

parameters.name: parameters
parameters.description:
Parameters whose `.grad` should be clipped.  Entries with
`grad is None` are skipped.

parameters.name: clip_value
parameters.description:
Symmetric magnitude bound.  Must be non-negative; gradients are
clamped to `[-clip_value, +clip_value]`.

returns.description: The clipping happens in place via `Parameter.grad`.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import clip_grad_value_
>>> # after loss.backward() ...
>>> clip_grad_value_(model.parameters(), clip_value=0.5)
notes:
The element-wise update is



$$
g_i \;\mapsto\; \mathrm{clip}(g_i,\, -c,\, +c),
$$

with $c = \text{clip\_value}$.  Because each component is
treated independently the direction of the gradient vector is *not*
preserved — large coordinates are flattened toward zero while small
ones pass through unchanged.

members.name: get_total_norm
members.signature: get_total_norm(parameters: Iterable[Parameter], norm_type: float = 2.0, error_if_nonfinite: bool = False, foreach: bool | None = None)
members.summary: Compute the global gradient $\ell_p$ norm without clipping.
members.extended:
Mirrors the measurement step of `clip_grad_norm_` but returns
the magnitude without rescaling any gradients — handy for logging
the gradient size every step regardless of whether you intend to
clip.

parameters.name: parameters
parameters.description:
Parameters whose gradients participate in the norm computation.
Entries with `grad is None` are skipped; the empty case
returns a zero tensor.

parameters.name: norm_type
parameters.description:
Order $p$ of the norm.  Default `2.0`; use `math.inf`
for the max-norm.

parameters.name: error_if_nonfinite
parameters.description: Raise on `inf` / `nan` results instead of returning them.

parameters.name: foreach
parameters.description:
Accepted for API compatibility; ignored — Lucid processes the
parameters sequentially through the C++ engine.

returns.description: Scalar tensor with the combined gradient norm.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils.clip_grad import get_total_norm
>>> g_norm = get_total_norm(model.parameters())
notes:
The returned scalar is



$$
\|g\|_p \;=\; \left(\sum_i |g_i|^p\right)^{1/p}
$$

taken over every element of every gradient — i.e. the same quantity
that `clip_grad_norm_` thresholds against `max_norm`.

members.name: parameters_to_vector
members.signature: parameters_to_vector(parameters: Iterable[Parameter])
members.summary: Flatten an iterable of parameters into a single 1-D `Tensor`.
members.extended:
Every parameter is detached, reshaped to 1-D, and concatenated in
iteration order.  Useful when an algorithm needs to view the whole
parameter set as one vector — most commonly second-order optimisers
(L-BFGS, conjugate-gradient, trust-region methods), hyper-gradient
solvers, and natural-gradient pre-conditioners.

parameters.name: parameters
parameters.description:
Parameters to flatten.  Each must be a `lucid._tensor.tensor.Tensor`
(or subclass).  All entries must share device and dtype; a mismatch
raises `ValueError`.

returns.description:
1-D tensor of length $\sum_k \text{numel}(p_k)$ on the same
device / dtype as the first parameter.  `requires_grad` is
`False` — the result is a detached snapshot, not a graph node.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import parameters_to_vector
>>> vec = parameters_to_vector(model.parameters())
>>> vec.shape
(n_params,)
notes:
Given parameters $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_K$ with sizes $n_k$,
the returned vector is



$$
v \;=\; \bigl[\,\operatorname{vec}(p_1);\,
\operatorname{vec}(p_2);\, \dots;\,
\operatorname{vec}(p_K)\,\bigr] \in \mathbb{R}^{\sum_k n_k}.
$$

The inverse operation is `vector_to_parameters`, which copies a
1-D tensor of the same length back into the original parameter shapes.

members.name: vector_to_parameters
members.signature: vector_to_parameters(vec: Tensor, parameters: Iterable[Parameter])
members.summary: Copy a flat 1-D tensor back into the supplied parameters in place.
members.extended:
Inverse of `parameters_to_vector`.  `vec` is sliced according
to the size of each parameter (in iteration order) and the slices are
written into the parameters' storage.  Typically called by line-search
routines and second-order optimisers that operate on the concatenated
vector and need to push the result back into the model.

parameters.name: vec
parameters.description:
1-D source tensor.  Length must equal $\sum_k \text{numel}(p_k)$
for the supplied parameter iterator; mismatches raise
`ValueError`.

parameters.name: parameters
parameters.description:
Destination parameters.  Each parameter's storage is overwritten
with the matching slice of `vec`, reshaped to the parameter's
own shape.

returns.description: The copy is performed in place; no value is returned.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import parameters_to_vector, vector_to_parameters
>>> v = parameters_to_vector(model.parameters())
>>> v = v + lr * search_direction
>>> vector_to_parameters(v, model.parameters())
notes:
Conceptually the operation splits `vec` at the parameter
boundaries and reshapes each chunk:



$$
p_k \;\leftarrow\; \operatorname{reshape}\bigl(
    v[s_{k-1}\!:\!s_k],\, \text{shape}(p_k)\bigr),
\qquad s_k = \sum_{j \leq k} n_j.
$$

Autograd graph state is left intact — the assignment touches storage
directly, not the parameter's identity, so existing references in
optimiser state remain valid.

members.name: remove_weight_norm
members.signature: remove_weight_norm(module: Module, name: str = 'weight')
members.summary: Reverse `weight_norm` and restore a plain leaf parameter.
members.extended:
Materialises $\mathbf{W} = g \cdot \mathbf{v}/\|\mathbf{v}\|$
one last time, writes the result back as a single leaf parameter
`module.<name>`, drops `<name>_g` / `<name>_v`, and detaches
the forward pre-hook.  Typical use is right before exporting a model
for inference where the reparametrised form is not needed.

parameters.name: module
parameters.description: Module previously passed through `weight_norm`.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description:
Attribute name of the parameter to un-normalise.  Must match the
`name` used at registration.  Default `"weight"`.

returns.description:
The same module, with `<name>` restored as a plain
`lucid.nn.parameter.Parameter` and the `g`/`v`
helpers removed.

raises.description: If no weight-norm registration exists on `<name>`.

examples:
>>> remove_weight_norm(layer)
>>> "weight_g" in dict(layer.named_parameters())
False
notes:
The materialised parameter is detached from the autograd graph — any
history accumulated through the reparametrisation is discarded.  If
you need the original `v` direction back, copy it out *before*
calling this.

members.name: weight_norm
members.signature: weight_norm(module: Module, name: str = 'weight', dim: int = 0)
members.summary: Reparametrise a weight as direction times magnitude (Salimans & Kingma 2016).
members.extended:
Replaces `module.<name>` with the derived tensor

$$
\mathbf{W} \;=\; g \,\cdot\, \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{v}\|}
$$

where `g` is a learnable scale (one entry per slice along `dim`)
and `v` is a learnable direction sharing the original weight's
shape.  Decoupling magnitude from direction often accelerates
convergence and improves conditioning, especially in deep
convolutional and recurrent networks.

parameters.name: module
parameters.description:
The module whose parameter is to be reparametrised.  Mutated in
place — the original leaf parameter is removed and replaced with
a derived tensor recomputed before every forward call.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Attribute name of the parameter to normalise.  Default `"weight"`.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Axis along which the scale `g` has its own entry; norms are
reduced over every other axis.  `0` (the default) is correct
for `Linear` and `Conv*` (one scale per output channel).
Use `-1` for output-row Linear layouts.

returns.description:
The same module, now carrying `<name>_g` and `<name>_v` as
leaf parameters in place of the original `<name>`.

examples:
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import weight_norm
>>> layer = weight_norm(nn.Linear(128, 64))
>>> layer.weight_g.shape, layer.weight_v.shape
((64, 1), (64, 128))
notes:
Two new parameters are registered:

* `<name>_g` — broadcast-shaped tensor (`1` along every axis
  except `dim`) holding the per-slice magnitudes.
* `<name>_v` — same shape as the original weight, holding the
  unnormalised direction.

A forward pre-hook recomputes $\mathbf{W} = g \cdot \mathbf{v}/\|\mathbf{v}\|$
so subsequent autograd / inference sees the same shape as before.
Invert with `remove_weight_norm`.

members.name: remove_spectral_norm
members.signature: remove_spectral_norm(module: Module, name: str = 'weight')
members.summary: Reverse `spectral_norm` and restore a plain leaf parameter.
members.extended:
Copies `module.<name>_orig` back into a fresh leaf parameter
`module.<name>`, removes the `<name>_u` / `<name>_v` power-
iteration buffers, and detaches the forward pre-hook.  Used before
exporting for inference when the normalisation overhead is no
longer wanted, or before fine-tuning without the spectral cap.

parameters.name: module
parameters.description: Module previously passed through `spectral_norm`.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Attribute name of the parameter to restore.  Default `"weight"`.

returns.description:
The same module, with `<name>` as a plain
`lucid.nn.parameter.Parameter` carrying the *unnormalised*
weight that was being trained behind the parametrisation.

raises.description: If no spectral-norm registration exists on `<name>`.

examples: >>> remove_spectral_norm(disc)
notes:
The restored parameter is $\mathbf{W}_{\text{orig}}$ — *not*
the rescaled $\mathbf{W}_{\text{orig}} / \sigma$ that was
visible during training.  If you want the normalised matrix in the
final checkpoint, copy it out *before* calling this function.

members.name: spectral_norm
members.signature: spectral_norm(module: Module, name: str = 'weight', n_power_iterations: int = 1, eps: float = 1e-12, dim: int | None = None)
members.summary: Constrain a weight's spectral norm via power iteration (Miyato et al. 2018).
members.extended:
Reparametrises `module.<name>` as

$$
\mathbf{W} \;=\; \frac{\mathbf{W}_{\text{orig}}}{\sigma(\mathbf{W}_{\text{orig}})},
$$

where $\sigma(\mathbf{W})$ is the largest singular value
(operator 2-norm) of the weight viewed as a matrix.  This bounds the
layer's Lipschitz constant by `1` and is the canonical
stabilisation trick for GAN discriminators; it also sees use in
certified-robust classifiers and contractive Transformer variants.

parameters.name: module
parameters.description:
The module whose parameter is to be normalised.  Mutated in
place.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Attribute name of the parameter.  Default `"weight"`.

parameters.name: n_power_iterations
parameters.description:
Number of power-iteration updates of the `u` / `v` vectors
performed before each training-mode forward.  Defaults to `1`
— sufficient because the state is preserved between calls and
converges over the course of training.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Numerical floor added inside the $\ell_2$ normalisation of
`u` and `v`.  Default `1e-12`.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Axis to treat as the row dimension of the flattened matrix.  If
`None` (the default), uses `0` for everything except
`ConvTranspose*` modules, which use `1` (so output channels
end up as rows).

returns.description:
The same module, with `<name>_orig` (the trainable underlying
matrix), `<name>_u` / `<name>_v` (buffers), and a forward
pre-hook performing the normalisation.

examples:
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import spectral_norm
>>> disc = spectral_norm(nn.Conv2d(3, 64, 3, padding=1))
notes:
Each forward in training mode runs `n_power_iterations` updates



$$
\mathbf{v} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{W}^{\!\top}\mathbf{u}}{\|\mathbf{W}^{\!\top}\mathbf{u}\|}, \qquad
\mathbf{u} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{W}\,\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{W}\,\mathbf{v}\|},
$$

estimates $\sigma \approx \mathbf{u}^{\!\top}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{v}$,
and exposes $\mathbf{W}_{\text{orig}} / \sigma$ as the active
weight.  In eval mode no updates occur — the cached buffers are used
as-is for deterministic inference.  Invert with
`remove_spectral_norm`.

members.name: PackedSequence
members.signature: PackedSequence()
members.summary: Compact representation of a batch of variable-length sequences.
members.extended:
A `PackedSequence` interleaves all surviving time-steps of every
sequence in the batch into a single flat tensor, dropping the
padding entries entirely.  RNN cells consume this form to avoid
wasting compute on padded positions.
examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils.rnn import pack_padded_sequence, pad_packed_sequence
>>> # padded (B=3, T=4, F=5), sorted-by-length [4, 3, 2]
>>> packed = pack_padded_sequence(x_padded, lengths=[4, 3, 2], batch_first=True)
>>> # ... feed to RNN ...
>>> unpacked, lengths = pad_packed_sequence(packed, batch_first=True)
notes:
Conceptually, with sequence lengths $\ell_1 \geq \dots \geq \ell_B$, the packed layout walks time-major:



$$
\text{data} = \bigl[\,x^{(1)}_0, \dots, x^{(B)}_0,\;
                    x^{(1)}_1, \dots, x^{(B_1)}_1,\;
                    \dots\,\bigr],
$$

where $B_t$ (= `batch_sizes[t]`) is the count of sequences
with length $> t$.

attributes.name: data
attributes.description:
Concatenation of the active features at each time-step in
descending-length order, shape `(sum(batch_sizes), *feat)`.

attributes.name: batch_sizes
attributes.description:
1-D int tensor giving the number of sequences still alive at
each successive time-step.  Strictly non-increasing.

attributes.name: sorted_indices
attributes.description:
Permutation that took the original batch order to the packed
(descending-length) order; `None` if the input was already
sorted.

attributes.name: unsorted_indices
attributes.description:
Inverse of `sorted_indices`; used by
`pad_packed_sequence` to restore the caller's original
batch order on unpack.

members.name: pack_padded_sequence
members.signature: pack_padded_sequence(input: Tensor, lengths: Tensor | list[int], batch_first: bool = False, enforce_sorted: bool = True)
members.summary: Pack a padded $(T, B, *)$ batch into a `PackedSequence`.
members.extended:
Strips out the padding cells so downstream RNN kernels iterate only
over genuine time-steps.  Reduces both compute and (with masked
losses) accidental gradient flow through pad positions.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description:
Padded batch.  Default layout is `(T, B, *)` with time on
axis 0; set `batch_first=True` for `(B, T, *)`.

parameters.name: lengths
parameters.description:
Per-sequence true lengths.  Shape `(B,)`.  When passed as a
tensor it must be a 1-D integer tensor.

parameters.name: batch_first
parameters.description: Whether `input` is laid out batch-first.  Default `False`.

parameters.name: enforce_sorted
parameters.description:
If `True` (the default), assume the caller already supplied
sequences in descending-length order — cheap but raises
`ValueError` on violation.  Set to `False` to have the
function sort internally; the sort permutation is stored on the
returned `PackedSequence` so `pad_packed_sequence`
can undo it.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils.rnn import pack_padded_sequence
>>> packed = pack_padded_sequence(x, lengths=[5, 3, 2], batch_first=False)
notes:
Total packed length equals $\sum_b \ell_b$, the sum of true
sequence lengths — strictly less than $T \cdot B$ whenever any
sequence is shorter than the max.

members.name: pack_sequence
members.signature: pack_sequence(sequences: list[Tensor], enforce_sorted: bool = True)
members.summary: One-shot pack-from-list — equivalent to `pad_sequence` then `pack_padded_sequence`.
members.extended:
Convenience wrapper for the common case where a list of per-example
tensors needs to become a `PackedSequence` without going
through an intermediate padded tensor.  Each entry's leading axis
is treated as the time dimension; remaining axes carry the feature
shape (which must be identical across the list).

parameters.name: sequences
parameters.description:
Per-example tensors.  Leading axis is time; trailing axes are
the feature shape and must match across entries.

parameters.name: enforce_sorted
parameters.description:
If `True` (the default), the caller must supply sequences in
non-increasing length order; mismatches raise
`ValueError`.  `False` is *not* currently supported
and raises `NotImplementedError` — sort externally for
now.

returns.description: Packed view of the input list.

raises.description:
If `sequences` is empty, or if it is not sorted while
`enforce_sorted=True`.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils.rnn import pack_sequence
>>> packed = pack_sequence([long_seq, mid_seq, short_seq])
notes:
Implemented as `pad_sequence` followed by
`pack_padded_sequence`.  The intermediate padded tensor lives
only for the duration of the call.

members.name: pad_packed_sequence
members.signature: pad_packed_sequence(sequence: PackedSequence, batch_first: bool = False, padding_value: float = 0.0, total_length: int | None = None)
members.summary: Inverse of `pack_padded_sequence` — produce a padded tensor.
members.extended:
Re-inflates a `PackedSequence` into a dense
$(T_\text{max}, B, *)$ (or $(B, T_\text{max}, *)$)
tensor with the original batch order restored.  The freshly created
cells beyond each sequence's true length are filled with
`padding_value`.

parameters.name: sequence
parameters.description: Packed batch to unpack.

parameters.name: batch_first
parameters.description:
Output layout.  `False` (default) yields `(T, B, *)`,
`True` yields `(B, T, *)`.

parameters.name: padding_value
parameters.description: Fill value for padded entries.  Default `0.0`.

parameters.name: total_length
parameters.description:
If given, pad up to this length instead of the longest packed
sequence.  Useful when downstream code expects a fixed
`T_max` (e.g. fully-static export graphs).  Must be `>=` the
longest sequence.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils.rnn import pad_packed_sequence
>>> padded, lens = pad_packed_sequence(packed, batch_first=True)
notes:
Output time dimension is $\max_b \ell_b$ (or `total_length`
if larger).  The function honours `unsorted_indices` on the
packed sequence so the rows of the returned tensor match the order
the caller used at pack time.

members.name: pad_sequence
members.signature: pad_sequence(sequences: list[Tensor], batch_first: bool = False, padding_value: float = 0.0)
members.summary: Stack a list of variable-length tensors into a padded batch.
members.extended:
Each input is padded along its leading time dimension up to the
longest sequence in `sequences`, then the padded versions are
stacked along a new batch axis.  Useful as the first step of an
RNN training loop when sequences are produced one at a time (per-
example tokenisation, audio framing, etc.).

parameters.name: sequences
parameters.description:
Non-empty list of per-example tensors.  Each must share the
same trailing feature shape and dtype / device; only the
leading length axis may differ.

parameters.name: batch_first
parameters.description:
Output layout.  `False` (default) yields `(T_max, B, *feat)`,
`True` yields `(B, T_max, *feat)`.

parameters.name: padding_value
parameters.description: Fill value for padded entries.  Default `0.0`.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils.rnn import pad_sequence
>>> batch = pad_sequence([a, b, c], batch_first=True)
notes:
Padding is constructed by concatenating a freshly allocated tail
of shape `(T_max - T_i, *feat)` onto each input — this keeps the
operation differentiable end-to-end (view-based mutation would
silently drop gradients through Lucid's autograd-aware
`lucid._tensor.tensor.Tensor`).

members.name: copy_parameters_and_buffers
members.signature: copy_parameters_and_buffers(source: Module, dest: Module)
members.summary: Copy all parameter / buffer values from `source` into `dest`.
members.extended:
Both modules must expose the same set of parameter and buffer names;
a `KeyError` is raised if a name found on `source` is missing on
`dest`.  Tensor data is copied in-place via `Tensor.detach()` and
storage assignment so that `dest`'s autograd graph (if any) is not
disturbed by the copy.

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: Module whose parameters / buffers provide the values to copy.

parameters.name: dest
parameters.description: Module whose parameters / buffers receive the copied values.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import copy_parameters_and_buffers
>>> src = MyModel()
>>> dst = MyModel()
>>> copy_parameters_and_buffers(src, dst)   # dst now mirrors src's weights + buffers
notes:
Shape and dtype of corresponding entries must match.  Devices may
differ — the source values are routed through `.to(device)` to
land on `dest`'s device first.

members.name: fuse_conv_bn_eval
members.signature: fuse_conv_bn_eval(conv: object, bn: object)
members.summary: Fold a BatchNorm layer into the preceding Conv weights (inference-only).
members.extended:
Because BN at eval time is an affine map with frozen statistics, the
composition `BN(Conv(x))` is itself a single convolution — the BN
can be analytically absorbed into the conv's weight and bias.  The
fused module computes exactly the same output but with one fewer
kernel launch and one fewer allocation per call.  Standard step in
deployment / quantisation pipelines.

parameters.name: conv
parameters.description: Convolution module whose weight / bias will absorb the BN.

parameters.name: bn
parameters.description:
BatchNorm whose rank matches `conv`.  Must be in eval mode (or
otherwise be using its frozen `running_mean` /
`running_var`); calling on a training-mode graph silently
yields wrong outputs.

returns.description:
Deep copy of `conv` with fused parameters.  Originals are not
mutated.

raises.description: If `conv` or `bn` is not one of the supported types.

examples:
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import fuse_conv_bn_eval
>>> conv = nn.Conv2d(3, 16, 3); bn = nn.BatchNorm2d(16)
>>> conv.eval(); bn.eval()
>>> fused = fuse_conv_bn_eval(conv, bn)
notes:
Let $\mu$, $\sigma^2$, $\epsilon$ be the BN running
statistics and $\gamma$, $\beta$ its affine parameters
(treated as $1$ and $0$ if `affine=False`).  The
fused weight and bias are



$$
\mathbf{W}_{\text{fused}} \;=\;
    \mathbf{W} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}},
$$



$$
b_{\text{fused}} \;=\;
    \frac{\gamma\,(b - \mu)}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta,
$$

where the scale broadcasts along the output-channel axis.  When the
original conv has no bias, $b$ is taken as $0$ and the
fused module gains one.

members.name: fuse_conv_bn_weights
members.signature: fuse_conv_bn_weights(conv_w: Tensor, conv_b: Tensor | None, bn_rm: Tensor, bn_rv: Tensor, bn_eps: float, bn_w: Tensor | None, bn_b: Tensor | None)
members.summary: Low-level form of `fuse_conv_bn_eval` operating on raw tensors.
members.extended:
Takes the relevant Conv and BN tensors as plain arguments and
returns the fused `(weight, bias)` pair.  Useful for build tools
that walk a serialised graph (ONNX, ahead-of-time compilation) and
need to perform the fusion without instantiating Module objects.

parameters.name: conv_w
parameters.description: Convolution weight, shape `(out_channels, in_channels, *kernel)`.

parameters.name: conv_b
parameters.description:
Convolution bias, shape `(out_channels,)`, or `None` if the
conv has no bias.

parameters.name: bn_rm
parameters.description: BatchNorm running mean, shape `(out_channels,)`.

parameters.name: bn_rv
parameters.description: BatchNorm running variance, shape `(out_channels,)`.

parameters.name: bn_eps
parameters.description: BatchNorm numerical-stability epsilon.

parameters.name: bn_w
parameters.description:
BatchNorm affine weight $\gamma$, or `None` if
`affine=False`.

parameters.name: bn_b
parameters.description: BatchNorm affine bias $\beta$, or `None`.

returns.description: Fused `(weight, bias)` on the same dtype / device as `conv_w`.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import fuse_conv_bn_weights
>>> W, b = fuse_conv_bn_weights(
...     conv.weight, conv.bias,
...     bn.running_mean, bn.running_var, bn.eps,
...     bn.weight, bn.bias,
... )
notes:
Identical math to `fuse_conv_bn_eval`:



$$
\mathbf{W}_{\text{fused}} = \mathbf{W}
    \cdot \gamma / \sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}, \qquad
b_{\text{fused}} = \gamma (b - \mu)/\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon} + \beta.
$$

members.name: fuse_linear_bn_eval
members.signature: fuse_linear_bn_eval(linear: object, bn: object)
members.summary: Fold a BatchNorm1d into the preceding Linear weights (inference-only).
members.extended:
The 1-D analogue of `fuse_conv_bn_eval` — absorbs the BN's
eval-time affine transform into a Linear layer's weight and bias.
The fused linear computes `BN(Linear(x))` exactly while using one
fewer kernel.

parameters.name: linear
parameters.description: Linear layer to absorb the BN into.

parameters.name: bn
parameters.description:
BatchNorm whose feature dimension matches `linear.out_features`.
Must be in eval mode (or otherwise be using its frozen
statistics).

returns.description:
Deep copy of `linear` with fused parameters.  Originals are
not mutated.

raises.description:
If `linear` is not `lucid.nn.modules.linear.Linear` or
`bn` is not `lucid.nn.modules.normalization.BatchNorm1d`.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import fuse_linear_bn_eval
>>> linear = nn.Linear(128, 64); bn = nn.BatchNorm1d(64)
>>> linear.eval(); bn.eval()
>>> fused = fuse_linear_bn_eval(linear, bn)
notes:
Math is identical to the conv case — the Linear's weight matrix is
scaled row-wise by $\gamma / \sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}$ and
the bias absorbs the mean shift and BN bias.

members.name: fuse_linear_bn_weights
members.signature: fuse_linear_bn_weights(linear_w: Tensor, linear_b: Tensor | None, bn_rm: Tensor, bn_rv: Tensor, bn_eps: float, bn_w: Tensor | None, bn_b: Tensor | None)
members.summary: Low-level form of `fuse_linear_bn_eval` operating on raw tensors.
members.extended:
Internally identical to `fuse_conv_bn_weights` — a Linear's
weight is just a 2-D conv weight from the BN's perspective.
Exposed under its own name to keep call sites self-documenting in
graph-rewrite passes.

parameters.name: linear_w
parameters.description: Linear weight matrix, shape `(out_features, in_features)`.

parameters.name: linear_b
parameters.description: Linear bias, shape `(out_features,)`, or `None`.

parameters.name: bn_rm
parameters.description: BatchNorm1d running mean.

parameters.name: bn_rv
parameters.description: BatchNorm1d running variance.

parameters.name: bn_eps
parameters.description: BatchNorm numerical-stability epsilon.

parameters.name: bn_w
parameters.description: BatchNorm affine $\gamma$.

parameters.name: bn_b
parameters.description: BatchNorm affine $\beta$.

returns.description:
Fused `(weight, bias)` on the same dtype / device as
`linear_w`.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import fuse_linear_bn_weights
>>> W, b = fuse_linear_bn_weights(
...     linear.weight, linear.bias,
...     bn.running_mean, bn.running_var, bn.eps,
...     bn.weight, bn.bias,
... )
notes:
Math:



$$
\mathbf{W}_{\text{fused}} = \mathbf{W}
    \cdot \gamma / \sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}, \qquad
b_{\text{fused}} = \gamma (b - \mu)/\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon} + \beta.
$$

members.name: skip_init
members.signature: skip_init(module_cls: type, args: object = (), kwargs: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a module while skipping its parameter initialisation.
members.extended:
Instantiates `module_cls(*args, **kwargs)` and then immediately
replaces every learnable parameter with an *uninitialised* buffer
of the same shape / dtype / device.  The intended pattern is
"construct, then `load_state_dict`": skipping the
`xavier_uniform` / `kaiming_normal` work that is about
to be overwritten saves measurable time on large models with many
layers.

parameters.name: module_cls
parameters.description: A `lucid.nn.Module` subclass to instantiate.

parameters.name: *args
parameters.description: Positional arguments forwarded to `module_cls.__init__`.

parameters.name: **kwargs
parameters.description: Keyword arguments forwarded to `module_cls.__init__`.

returns.description:
Fully constructed module whose parameter data is undefined.
Buffers (running statistics, attention masks, etc.) are left
intact because they are typically not refreshed from a
checkpoint.

examples:
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> from lucid.nn.utils import skip_init
>>> model = skip_init(nn.Linear, 1024, 1024)
>>> # ... immediately load a checkpoint ...
notes:
Lucid allocates each parameter normally, then swaps its underlying
storage for the output of `lucid.empty` — semantically
equivalent to the reference framework's `meta`-device trick.
Callers **must not** inspect parameter values before loading a
checkpoint; the contents are unspecified and may contain `nan`
or `inf`.

summary: lucid.nn.utils: utility functions for neural networks.
